w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Sales reporting is one of the most important activities in any business because it helps an organization understand how products are moving, how customers are responding, and whether the business is meeting its revenue targets. In simple terms, sales reporting involves collecting sales data, organizing it, summarizing it, and presenting it in a form that can support managerial decision-making. For many years, organizations relied on manual methods such as spreadsheet entries, printed summaries, and periodic verbal updates to prepare sales reports. Although these methods were useful in the early stages of business operations, they often became slow, repetitive, and prone to human error as the volume of sales data increased. In a business environment where competition is high and customer demand changes quickly, organizations can no longer depend only on delayed and static reports to understand sales trends. This is why sales reporting has gradually become more closely linked with data analytics, business intelligence, and dashboard-based systems that can present information in a clearer and more timely manner.

The growing importance of data analytics in reporting has changed the way businesses use information. Instead of just storing sales records, organizations now want to analyze patterns, compare performance across periods, and identify factors affecting sales outcomes. Data analytics makes this possible by transforming raw transactional data into meaningful insight that managers can act on. In the studies reviewed, the use of analytics is presented as a major support for better forecasting, more effective monitoring, and stronger decision-making in business settings. For example, sales analytics has been discussed as a way of improving the understanding of sales performance and helping managers respond more intelligently to business changes (

2020). Likewise, BI-based dashboard studies show that once data is properly processed and visualized, decision-makers are able to see trends, compare performance, and follow up on business issues much faster than with traditional reporting approaches (Kallel et al., 2024;

Dashboards and business intelligence tools have become central to modern reporting systems because they make it possible to present large amounts of data in a simple visual form. A dashboard gives users a visual summary of important indicators, often through charts, graphs, KPI cards,

trend lines, and filters that allow them to interact with the data. This is especially useful in sales environments where managers need to monitor revenue, quantity sold, profit, discounts, customers, and regional performance at a glance. Power BI and Tableau are among the most commonly discussed tools in recent dashboard studies because they support interactive reporting and make it easier to build visual sales analysis systems without requiring users to interpret raw tables alone (Kallel et al., 2024;

2025). In addition, OLAP concepts are important in sales reporting because they allow data to be viewed from different dimensions such as time, product, branch, customer segment, or sales region. ETL processes also play an essential role by extracting data from source systems, transforming it into a clean and useful format, and loading it into a database or data warehouse for analysis. In this way, data warehousing, ETL, and OLAP work together to support a stronger reporting structure that can handle sales data more efficiently and consistently.

The need for real-time sales insight has also become more pressing in modern organizations. Sales managers do not only want to know what happened last month or last quarter; they often need to see what is happening now so that they can respond quickly to performance changes. Real-time or near real-time reporting helps managers detect sales declines, identify top-performing products, track targets, and follow customer behavior while the business is still active. Studies on BI and dashboard implementation have shown that timely visibility into performance indicators improves decision-making and makes management reporting more useful (

2024). Because of this, there is a strong need to design and implement a sales reporting system using data analytics so that businesses can move beyond manual reporting and gain access to interactive, accurate, and timely sales information.

Despite the importance of sales information, many organizations still rely on manual or semi-manual methods for preparing sales reports. These methods often require staff to collect data from multiple sources, clean it manually, enter it into spreadsheets, and then prepare summaries for management review. This process is usually slow and stressful, especially where sales records are large or come from different branches, departments, or platforms. As a result, report generation is delayed, and by the time the report reaches management, the information may no longer reflect the current business situation. This delay weakens the usefulness of the report and may lead to late responses to sales problems or missed opportunities for improvement.

Another major problem is inconsistency and inaccuracy in sales records. When data is entered manually, there is always the possibility of duplication, omission, formatting errors, or calculation mistakes. These errors can affect the quality of sales reports and reduce confidence in the information being used for decision-making. In many cases, different staff members may prepare reports using different assumptions or data formats, which makes it difficult to compare results consistently across time periods. The studies reviewed indicate that the quality of data and information has a direct effect on the usefulness of BI systems and reporting outputs, which means poor data quality can seriously weaken the reporting process itself (Norman et al., 2024). When sales information is inaccurate, managers may make decisions based on false trends, and this can affect revenue planning, stock control, and performance evaluation.

There is also the issue of limited visibility into key performance indicators. In a traditional reporting environment, KPI tracking is often done after the fact, usually at the end of a week, month, or quarter. This makes it difficult to identify emerging problems early. If sales targets are being missed, product categories are underperforming, or particular regions are declining, management may not notice quickly enough to intervene. The reviewed dashboard-based studies show that interactive reporting tools are useful because they make KPI monitoring easier and more immediate, especially when built around business needs such as sales volume, profit, customer trends, and order movement (Kallel et al., 2024;
2025). Without a real-time or near real-time dashboard, organizations remain dependent on static reports that cannot respond well to fast-changing business conditions.

Handling large sales data is another difficulty. As businesses grow, the amount of sales information increases rapidly, and manual tools often become too weak to manage it properly. Spreadsheet-based reporting can work for small datasets, but it becomes difficult to maintain when there are multiple product lines, several sales channels, and frequent transactions. In such cases, data warehouse structures, ETL processes, and BI tools become necessary because they are better suited for organizing and analyzing large volumes of data (

n.d.). Existing methods are therefore not enough because they cannot provide the speed, consistency, and analytical depth that modern sales reporting requires. This creates the need for a more reliable system that can automate report generation, support dashboard visualization, and improve the overall quality of sales decision-making.
1.3 Aim and Objectives of the Study

The aim of this study is to design and implement a sales reporting system using data analytics in order to improve the collection, processing, visualization, and interpretation of sales data for better managerial decision-making.
The objectives of the study are:
To examine the existing methods of sales reporting used in organizations.
To identify the major challenges associated with manual sales reporting systems.
To design a functional sales reporting system capable of processing sales records for analytical purposes.
To implement a structured database for storing and retrieving sales-related data efficiently.
To develop interactive dashboards for monitoring sales trends and key performance indicators.

This project focuses on the design and implementation of a sales reporting system that uses data analytics to support reporting, dashboarding, and KPI monitoring. The system is intended to handle sales-related data such as transaction records, customer information, product details, sales amounts, quantities sold, time-based sales trends, and performance indicators. The project covers the analysis of this data and the presentation of results through visual dashboards and structured reports that can assist managers in understanding business performance. It also includes the development of a database structure that can store the data in an organized manner and support reporting operations efficiently.

The scope of the study is limited to the core functions of sales reporting and does not extend into full enterprise resource planning, advanced machine learning forecasting, or deep customer relationship management integration. Although the system may support data transformation and analytical processing, it is not designed to replace all business systems used by the organization. The reports generated in this project are mainly focused on sales performance, sales trends, and key operational indicators rather than broader accounting or supply chain functions. In other words, the project is centered on reporting and visualization for sales analysis, with emphasis on dashboards, KPI display, and decision support rather than on every possible business process in the organization.

This study is important because it addresses a common problem in business operations: the difficulty of turning sales records into useful information quickly and accurately. Many organizations accumulate a large amount of sales data but do not always have an effective system for analyzing it in a timely way. By designing a data-driven sales reporting system, the project contributes to more efficient sales monitoring and stronger decision support. It helps show how data analytics can be applied in a real reporting environment to improve access to information, reduce reporting delays, and support better business planning.

The study will benefit managers because it provides them with a clearer view of sales performance, key trends, and areas that require attention. It will also benefit sales staff because it can make expectations and performance targets more visible, which may improve accountability and reporting discipline. For businesses, the project can help reduce manual reporting burden, improve reporting accuracy, and support faster reactions to sales changes. The reviewed literature shows that BI and dashboard systems can help organizations move from raw data to actionable insight, especially when KPI tracking and visualization are built into the reporting workflow (Kallel et al., 2024; Norman et al., 2024;
2025). For future researchers, the study provides a useful foundation for further work in sales intelligence, reporting automation, dashboard design, and data warehouse-based analytics.

The project also contributes to the broader field of business intelligence and reporting systems by showing how sales data can be organized, transformed, and presented in a way that supports real business needs. Since many organizations still depend on manual reporting, this work can serve as a practical example of how data analytics tools and BI concepts can improve everyday management processes. It therefore has both academic and practical value, especially for institutions and businesses that want to improve decision-making through data.

Sales reporting refers to the process of collecting, organizing, analyzing, and presenting sales information in a structured manner in order to understand business performance and support decision-making.
Data analytics is the process of examining raw data to identify patterns, relationships, and useful insights that can improve business decisions.
Business intelligence refers to the technologies and methods used to collect, analyze, and present business data for effective decision-making.
A dashboard is a visual interface that presents important information through charts, tables, graphs, and indicators for easy understanding.
A KPI is a measurable value used to determine how effectively an organization is achieving its goals.
ETL means Extract, Transform, and Load. It is the process of collecting data from different sources, preparing it, and storing it for reporting and analysis.
OLAP stands for Online Analytical Processing. It is a method used to analyze data from multiple dimensions such as time, product, or location.
A data warehouse is a centralized storage system used to keep large amounts of organized data for reporting and analysis.
Real-time reporting refers to the ability of a system to provide updated information immediately after data is entered.
Sales performance refers to the extent to which sales activities meet business goals or targets.

This chapter reviews the literature related to the design and implementation of a sales reporting system using data analytics. The review is organized around the major concepts that support the study, including sales reporting, data analytics, business intelligence, dashboard systems, KPI monitoring, ETL, OLAP, data warehousing, and the practical use of Power BI and Tableau in reporting environments. The purpose of the review is to show how previous studies have approached sales analysis and reporting, what tools and methods they used, and where the current study fits into the existing body of knowledge. From the literature, it is clear that organizations are increasingly moving away from manual reporting and toward automated, data-driven reporting systems that support faster decisions and better visibility of sales performance (Kallel et al., 2024;

The reviewed studies also show that sales reporting is no longer treated as a simple record-keeping task. Instead, it has become a management function that depends on structured data, reliable databases, visual dashboards, and analytical tools that can process large volumes of information. Studies on sales analytics and BI implementation show that organizations need systems that can integrate transactional data, track KPIs, and present results in a way that non-technical decision-makers can easily understand (
Norman et al., 2024). This chapter therefore connects the theoretical ideas behind reporting systems with the practical findings of the empirical studies already collected for the project.

Sales reporting refers to the systematic process of gathering sales-related data and presenting it in a structured way so that performance can be monitored and evaluated. In a business context, sales reports usually contain information such as total sales, product performance, revenue trends, customer purchasing patterns, regional performance, and target achievement. The main purpose of sales reporting is not just to record sales transactions but to help management understand how the organization is performing over time. In the literature, sales reporting is often linked with decision support, performance analysis, and business improvement because it gives managers a clearer picture of what is happening in the sales environment (Kallel et al., 2024;
The reviewed studies show that sales reporting has gradually become more visual and analytical. Instead of long tables and static summaries, businesses now use dashboards and BI tools to present

sales information more clearly. This shift is important because sales data often comes from different sources and can be too large or too complex to interpret manually. A well-designed sales reporting system therefore serves as a bridge between raw data and managerial decision-making, turning numbers into meaningful insights that support action (
Liu, Q., et al., 2022Sharda, et al., :2024
2.3 Importance of Sales Reporting in Organizations

Sales reporting is important because it helps organizations understand whether they are meeting their business goals. For managers, it provides evidence of what is working and what is not working in sales operations. It also helps organizations identify top-selling products, weak product lines, high-performing regions, and changes in customer demand. Without proper reporting, management may depend on guesswork or incomplete information, which can lead to poor planning and weak responses to business problems. The literature shows that reporting systems and dashboards improve transparency and allow organizations to make more informed decisions, especially when KPI monitoring is included in the reporting process (Kallel et al., 2024; Norman et al., 2024).

Sales reporting is also important because it supports accountability. Sales staff can be evaluated more fairly when their performance is measured against clear indicators such as revenue, volume, conversion rate, and target achievement. In addition, reporting helps organizations detect patterns early, such as declining sales in one branch or sudden drops in a product category. This makes it possible to respond quickly rather than waiting until the end of a reporting period. In practical terms, sales reporting improves coordination between sales teams and management, and it also creates a stronger foundation for strategic planning and performance review (

Traditional sales reporting methods usually involve manual compilation of sales records using spreadsheets, paper files, and periodic summaries prepared by staff. In many organizations, these reports are created weekly, monthly, or quarterly, depending on the reporting cycle. The data is often collected from different departments or branches and then manually entered into a spreadsheet before it is summarized for management. This approach was common before the spread of BI tools and data visualization platforms, and it is still used in some small or moderately structured organizations. However, the literature suggests that such methods are increasingly
inadequate in modern business environments where sales data is larger, more dynamic, and more distributed than before (
Liu, Q., et al., 2022Sharda, et al., :2024

Traditional reporting methods can still be useful in simple settings, but they do not provide the level of speed, accuracy, or interactivity that modern organizations need. The reports are usually static, meaning that users cannot drill down into the data or explore trends interactively. Also, because the process depends heavily on human input, there is a higher chance of delays and errors. Studies that focus on dashboard-based reporting show that moving from manual summaries to digital BI reporting improves the usefulness of sales information and makes reporting less stressful for staff (Kallel et al., 2024;
2.5 Challenges of Manual Sales Reporting Systems

Manual sales reporting systems have several weaknesses that make them unsuitable for organizations dealing with large or changing sales data. One major challenge is delay. Because data must be collected, checked, and entered by hand, reports are usually produced after the relevant decisions may already have been made. This reduces the value of the report because management is acting on old information. Another issue is error. Manual reporting often leads to duplication, missing values, inconsistent formats, and calculation mistakes. When these problems occur, the credibility of the report is reduced and decision-making becomes less reliable. The literature on BI and data-driven decision-making shows that poor data quality can affect the effectiveness of reporting systems and reduce the usefulness of the insights produced (Norman et al., 2024).

Another challenge is the difficulty of handling large datasets. As sales records increase across branches, products, and time periods, spreadsheet-based methods become harder to manage. They may become slow, difficult to update, and difficult to audit. In addition, manual reports often lack real-time visibility, which means management only sees performance after a delay. This is a serious weakness for organizations that need quick responses to sales trends. The studies reviewed on Power BI and Tableau dashboards show that automated reporting systems are preferred because they can process data faster and provide visual summaries that are easier to interpret than manually prepared tables (Kallel et al., 2024;

Data analytics is the process of examining data in order to identify patterns, relationships, trends, and useful insights that can support decisions. In a sales environment, data analytics can be used

to study revenue movement, customer behavior, product performance, and seasonal trends. It is more than simple calculation because it involves interpreting data in a way that helps users understand what the figures mean. The reviewed literature describes sales analytics as an important part of modern decision-making because it helps businesses turn large volumes of sales data into actionable intelligence (

Data analytics is often divided into descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive forms, although not every study uses all four categories. In the context of this project, the most relevant forms are descriptive and diagnostic analytics, since the system is intended to summarize sales performance and explain trends through dashboards and reports. The use of analytics tools such as Power BI and Tableau makes this process easier because they allow users to analyze data visually and interactively rather than relying only on manual calculations. As the literature shows, analytics is one of the key foundations of business intelligence and reporting systems (Kallel et al., 2024;
2.7 Role of Data Analytics in Sales Reporting

Data analytics plays a central role in sales reporting because it makes reports more meaningful, timely, and useful. Rather than simply listing transactions, analytics helps the reporting system identify what the data is saying about business performance. For example, it can show which product is selling best, which region is underperforming, or whether sales are improving over time. This is very important because management often needs not only records but also interpretation. The studies reviewed indicate that data analytics supports better forecasting, better monitoring, and more informed management review processes, especially when combined with dashboard tools and KPIs (
Liu, Q., et al., 2022Liu, Q., et al., 2022

In practical terms, analytics improves sales reporting by allowing organizations to move from passive record-keeping to active decision support. It also enables comparisons across time periods and business units, which helps managers detect changes early. A report generated through analytics can show trends such as monthly sales growth or product-level profit margins in a way that is much easier to understand than raw data tables. This is why sales reporting systems increasingly depend on analytics as the core mechanism for producing insight, not just data summaries.

Business intelligence refers to the use of technologies and processes for collecting, organizing, analyzing, and presenting business data to support decision-making. In sales reporting, BI is important because it connects data storage with analysis and visualization. The literature reviewed shows that BI systems improve reporting quality by integrating data from different sources and presenting them in a structured format that users can understand quickly. For example, studies on BI usage found that the quality of the data, the system, and the information itself strongly affects how useful the BI system becomes for management accounting and performance review (Norman et al., 2024).

Decision-making becomes more effective when business intelligence is properly implemented because managers are able to see both the current state of sales and the direction of change. BI tools support decisions about targets, stock, pricing, promotions, and market focus. In the reviewed case studies, dashboards built with BI tools helped decision-makers analyze performance and prepare management reports more efficiently than traditional methods (Kallel et al., 2024;
). This shows that business intelligence is not only a technical concept but also a practical management tool that improves organizational responsiveness.

A dashboard system is a visual interface that displays key information from a dataset in a compact and interactive way. In sales reporting, dashboards are used to present indicators such as total sales, profit, target progress, regional trends, customer counts, and product performance. The aim is to give users a quick overview of the business situation without forcing them to study large tables of data. Dashboard systems are especially useful because they combine visual clarity with interactivity, allowing users to filter by time, product, region, or other dimensions. The studies reviewed repeatedly show that dashboards make reporting more effective because they simplify complex information and support ongoing monitoring (Kallel et al., 2024;

Dashboards are important not only for presentation but also for analysis. A good dashboard can help a manager notice a decline in sales, a spike in demand, or a change in customer preference. In this sense, the dashboard is not just a display tool but also a management tool. When it is connected to a structured database and supported by ETL and OLAP processes, it becomes part of a larger reporting system that supports regular decision-making. The use of Power BI and Tableau in many of the reviewed studies demonstrates how dashboard systems have become central to modern business reporting.
2.10 Importance of Dashboard Visualization in Sales Reporting

Dashboard visualization is important because it makes sales data easier to understand at a glance. Humans generally understand patterns, trends, and comparisons faster when information is shown visually rather than as large rows of numbers. This is one reason dashboards have become popular in business intelligence and reporting systems. In the reviewed studies, dashboard visualization helped managers observe trends in sales success, KPI movement, and performance variation across different business units (

For sales reporting, visualization is especially useful because sales data changes over time and often needs to be compared across categories. Charts, graphs, heatmaps, and trend lines can reveal seasonal patterns, weak markets, and high-performing products more quickly than manual tables. Visualization also makes reports more accessible to non-technical users, which is important in many organizations where decision-makers may not have the time or skill to work through complex raw data. As a result, dashboard visualization improves both understanding and action, which is why it is central to the proposed system.
2.11 Key Performance Indicators in Sales Reporting

Key Performance Indicators, or KPIs, are measurable values used to assess the performance of a business activity. In sales reporting, KPIs may include total revenue, total orders, average sales value, profit margin, growth rate, conversion rate, target achievement, and customer retention. KPIs are important because they provide a clear basis for measuring success instead of relying on general impressions. The reviewed literature shows that KPI selection is a major part of dashboard design because the usefulness of the reporting system depends on whether it tracks the right business indicators (Kallel et al., 2024;

In a sales reporting system, KPIs help users focus on the most important measures rather than being distracted by unnecessary data. For instance, a sales manager may want to know not only how much was sold, but also whether the sales were profitable, whether targets were met, and how performance differs by region or product. Dashboard studies reviewed in this work show that KPI-based reporting helps organizations develop clearer management reviews and more focused improvement plans. Therefore, KPI monitoring is one of the strongest practical reasons for implementing a sales reporting system using data analytics.

A data warehouse is a centralized repository that stores integrated data from different sources in a way that supports analysis and reporting. It is not mainly designed for day-to-day transaction processing; rather, it is meant for querying, summarizing, and analyzing historical and current business data. In sales reporting systems, a data warehouse helps to combine information from sources such as sales platforms, customer records, and product databases into one structured environment. The literature reviewed points to the importance of data marts and warehouse-style structures in dashboard development because they make the reporting process more efficient and reliable (Kallel et al., 2024;).

Data warehousing is important because sales data is often scattered across multiple operational systems. Without a warehouse, it can be difficult to bring all the information together in a consistent format. A warehouse also supports historical analysis, which is necessary when managers want to compare sales across months, quarters, or years. Since reporting systems often depend on both current and past data, the warehouse becomes the foundation for stable analytics and dashboard performance. This makes it a key concept in the present study.

ETL stands for Extract, Transform, and Load, and it is the process used to move data from source systems into a reporting database or warehouse. Extraction involves retrieving data from different sources; transformation involves cleaning, formatting, and restructuring the data; and loading involves storing it in the target system for analysis. ETL is very important in sales reporting because raw sales data may come in inconsistent formats, contain duplicate records, or require calculation before it can be used for reporting. The reviewed literature shows that data preparation is often necessary before dashboard development because the quality of the final report depends on the quality of the underlying data (Kallel et al., 2024).

In practical sales systems, ETL supports reliability and consistency. For example, data from branches or sales channels may need to be standardized so that comparisons are meaningful. This process may also involve removing errors, converting dates, calculating totals, and creating KPI fields. ETL therefore serves as the bridge between raw data and analytical reporting. Without it, the dashboard may show misleading information or fail to reflect the true state of the business. That is why ETL is widely recognized as one of the technical foundations of modern sales reporting systems.

Online Analytical Processing, or OLAP, is a technology that allows users to analyze data from multiple dimensions quickly and interactively. In sales reporting, OLAP makes it possible to view data by time, product, customer, branch, or region. This multidimensional structure is useful because sales performance is usually not understood from one angle alone. A manager may want to see monthly sales trends, regional comparisons, or product-level profit analysis, and OLAP supports this kind of flexible analysis. The reviewed BI literature suggests that analytical depth is a major advantage of structured warehouse and dashboard systems, especially when they are built for management reporting (

OLAP is valuable because it supports fast slicing, dicing, drilling down, and rolling up of data. These actions allow users to move between summary and detail with ease. In a sales dashboard, OLAP-style analysis can help users notice whether sales declines are caused by one product line, one location, or one time period. This kind of analysis is difficult to achieve with manual reporting alone. Therefore, OLAP remains a useful concept in the design of sales reporting systems that need both speed and analytical flexibility.

Real-time reporting systems are designed to provide updated information almost immediately after data is entered or processed. In sales environments, this can be very important because decisions often need to be made quickly. If a product is underperforming, if a branch is missing target, or if customer demand is changing, management may need to know right away rather than waiting for a month-end report. The literature reviewed emphasizes that modern BI and dashboard systems are increasingly used to reduce the delay between data generation and management insight (Kallel et al., 2024;

Real-time reporting improves responsiveness and makes reporting more relevant to current business conditions. It can also support better accountability, since performance is visible more quickly. However, real-time systems require good data management, clean databases, and strong integration between reporting tools and source systems. This makes them more complex than manual reporting, but also much more valuable for organizations that want to stay competitive. In sales reporting, real-time capability is therefore a major advantage of a well-designed data analytics system.

Existing sales reporting systems vary widely depending on the size and needs of the organization. Some are still built around spreadsheets and manually prepared summaries, while others use BI tools, dashboards, and data warehouse-backed reporting platforms. The literature reviewed shows a gradual movement toward integrated systems that combine databases, ETL processes, KPI monitoring, and visual dashboards. In the studies on Power BI and Tableau, the reporting systems were designed to support decision-making by providing quick access to sales indicators and graphical summaries (Kallel et al., 2024;

Even though many existing systems are useful, they often have limitations in scalability, automation, or real-time visibility. Some systems focus mainly on presentation but do not fully address data integration or consistency. Others may provide reports but lack flexibility for deeper analysis. This shows that there is still room for more complete sales reporting systems that combine proper database design, analytics, dashboarding, and reporting automation in one solution. The present study builds on this need by focusing on the design and implementation of a more integrated sales reporting environment.
Chen, H., Chiang, R. H. L., &
Business Intelligence and Analytics: From Big Data to Big Impact
Does not provide system-level implementation details
Include real-world implementation frameworks for BI systems
Sharda, R., Delen, D., & Turban, E.
Business Intelligence and Analytics: Systems for Decision Support
Lacks empirical validation in real organizations
Add case-based deployment in industry environments
Watson, H. J., & Wixom, B. H.
The Current State of Business Intelligence
Focuses on early BI systems, outdated context
Update with modern cloud-based BI tools like Power BI
Does not include data engineering or backend systems
Integrate dashboards with ETL and data warehouse systems
Add support for real-time streaming data warehouses
An Overview of Business Intelligence Technology
Include modern dashboard tools and cloud BI systems
Knowledge Management and Business Intelligence Integration
Apply to sales reporting systems with real datasets
Add technical BI pipeline design (ETL + dashboards)
Not focused on modern analytics systems
Wixom, B. H., & Watson, H. J.
Focuses on adoption, not system design
Add implementation architecture for BI systems
Performance Dashboards: Measuring, Monitoring, and Managing KPI Systems
Integrate OLAP and data warehouse modeling
Sivarajah, U., Kamal, M. M., Irani, Z., & Weerakkody, V.
Critical analysis of Big Data challenges
Does not focus specifically on sales reporting
Apply findings to sales KPI monitoring systems
Data Analytics for Decision Making in Business Systems

The table above shows that most studies concentrate on either dashboard implementation or BI visualization, while fewer studies combine database design, ETL, OLAP, and reporting automation into one complete sales reporting system. Several studies also remain limited by single-case settings, incomplete bibliographic details, or weak validation of the business impact. These weaknesses are important because they suggest that although sales dashboards are widely discussed, there is still a need for a more integrated and fully documented sales reporting system that connects the technical and managerial sides of the problem (Norman et al., 2024;).

The literature shows a clear movement toward the use of BI tools, dashboards, and sales analytics for reporting and decision-making, but several important gaps remain. First, many studies focus on one tool, such as Power BI or Tableau, without showing how the reporting system works from end to end, including database design, ETL, KPI logic, and user reporting flow. Second, a number of the available studies are case-specific and therefore difficult to generalize across different business settings. Third, while dashboards are widely discussed, fewer studies explain how real-time reporting, data warehousing, and OLAP can be combined into one practical and maintainable sales reporting system.

Another gap is that some studies emphasize visualization but pay less attention to report automation and data governance. In many organizations, the challenge is not only creating a dashboard but ensuring that the data feeding the dashboard is accurate, current, and consistently structured. The present study intends to fill this gap by designing and implementing a sales reporting system that integrates data analytics, structured database management, dashboard

visualization, KPI monitoring, and reporting automation in a more unified way. By doing this, the study aims to provide a practical model that is not limited to visual presentation alone but also addresses the reporting process behind the visuals.

The literature reviewed in this chapter shows that sales reporting has evolved from manual summaries into a more advanced, data-driven function supported by business intelligence tools, dashboards, ETL, OLAP, and data warehousing. The reviewed studies demonstrate that organizations need timely, accurate, and visually meaningful sales information if they are to make sound decisions in a competitive environment. They also show that Power BI and Tableau have become important tools for building interactive dashboards that can track KPIs and simplify reporting tasks.

At the same time, the review reveals that many existing approaches still have weaknesses, especially in terms of integration, real-time capability, and end-to-end reporting design. Some studies focus mainly on visualization, while others emphasize analytics without fully addressing the reporting architecture. These findings justify the present study, which seeks to design and implement a sales reporting system using data analytics in a way that combines database structure, dashboard presentation, KPI monitoring, and practical reporting support. In that sense, the current project is built directly on the strengths of the reviewed literature while responding to the gaps that remain in the field.
Chen, H., Chiang, R. H. L., &
, V. C. (2022). Business intelligence and analytics: From big data to big impact.
, V. (2021). An overview of business intelligence technology.
Competing on analytics: Updated perspectives for the AI era
. Boston, MA: Harvard Business Review Press.
Performance dashboards: Measuring, monitoring, and managing KPI systems
Information dashboard design: The effective visual communication of data
Herschel, R. T., & Jones, N. E. (2025). Knowledge management and business intelligence integration.
The data warehouse toolkit: The definitive guide to dimensional modeling
Kallel, A., et al. (2024). Developing a sales dashboard with Power BI: A case study in a pharmaceutical company.
International Journal of Business Intelligence Research, 15
Liu, Q., et al. (2022). Data analytics for decision making in business systems.
Meghana, P., et al. (2020). Sales analytics and decision-making in business environments.
Norman, T., et al. (2024). Determinants and consequences of business intelligence system usage by management accountants.
Power, D. J. (2023). Decision support systems: Concepts and evolution.
Sivarajah, U., Kamal, M. M., Irani, Z., & Weerakkody, V. (2021). Critical analysis of big data challenges.
Sharda, R., Delen, D., & Turban, E. (2024).
Business intelligence, analytics, and data science: A managerial perspective
Watson, H. J., & Wixom, B. H. (2020). The current state of business intelligence.
Wixom, B. H., & Watson, H. J. (2022). The BI-based organization: Key insights.
